coredata.dc:title: A highly sensitive flexible humidity sensor based on a wafer-level composite material of carbon-quantum-dots@nanofiber clusters
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Yuefang
Li, Mao
Zhou, Na
Xu, Shaohang
Shi, Meng
Huang, Chengjun
Mao, Haiyang
coredata.dc:description: A novel composite material composed of carbon-quantum-dots@nanofiber clusters (CQDs@NFCs) is successfully developed in wafer level, taking it as a sensitive material, a flexible humidity sensor is also developed. Since the composite material is superhydrophilic and contains a super large number of nanopores of different sizes, capillary condensation is able to take place within the composite material at different relative humidity (RH) conditions. Taking advantage of the chemi-physisorption and the capillary condensation, such a sensing material helps to significantly improve sensitivity of the flexible sensor, especially at low RH conditions. Compared with the device adopting only nanofibers as the sensing material, this novel sensor offers a 4.3-time increament in sensitivity within a humidity range of 7%−59% RH, while maintains high sensitivity in the entire humidity range. This heightened sensitivity is crucial for monitoring low-level humidity with high accuracy, making the sensor highly promising for various applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs@NFCs
Flexible humidity sensors
Capillary condensation
Humidity monitoring

Fabrication of humidity sensors based on the CQDs@NFCs
Morphologies of the NFs and the CQDs@NFCs
Performance of the humidity sensor based on CQDs@NFCs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported in part by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 62201567), Youth Innovation Promotion Association, Chinese Academy of Sciences (Grant No. 2018153 and 2022117), Key-Area Research and Development Program of Guangdong Province (Grant No. 2019B010117001), and State Key Laboratory of Dynamic Test Jointly Built by Province and Ministry Open Fund (Grant No. 2022-SYSJJ-07).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fabrication process for the humidity sensors adopting carbon quantum dots@nanofiber clusters (CQDs@NFCs) as the sensitive material.

(a) A photo of a nanoforest-based flexible humidity sensor (NFHS) prepared on a flexible substrate. (b) A SEM image of the nanoforests (NFs). (c) A photo of a sensor based on CQDs@NFCs prepared on a flexible substrate. (d) SEM images of the CQDs@NFCs (inset: the top of the CQDs@NFCs). (e) Top view of the CQDs@NFCs (inset: the bottom of the CQDs@NFCs). (f) A TEM image of the CQDs around a single nanofiber.

Performance of the humidity sensor based on CQDs@NFCs. (a) Real-time response to RH within a range of 7%−97% RH. (b) Responses of three different humidity sensors: the CQDs@NFCs humidity sensor, the NFHS, and the PFHS in a wide range of 7%−97% RH. (c) The linear fitting curves of a humidity sensor based on CQDs@NFCs. (d) Comparison between the sensitivities of PFHS, NFHS, and CQDs@NFCs humidity sensors within the humidity range of 7%−97% RH. (e) Five-cycle repeatability of the humidity sensor based on CQDs@NFCs in a loop test between 7% RH and 85% RH.
Chemisorption and physisorption of water molecules to CQDs@NFCs in a flexible humidity sensor.

(a-b) Capillary condensation in nanopores with various sizes and various shapes under different RH conditions. (c) The capillary condensation taking place within a structure of CQDs@NFC under various RH conditions.

(a) The HMI system based on the flexible humidity sensor using CQDs@NFCs. (b) Main components of the flexible circuit and logic block diagram of a wireless communication system. (c-f) Display of RH in a phone for different scenarios: ambient humidity, fingertip humidity, plant leaf humidity and diaper humidity. (g-i) Schematic illustration of a wireless control consisting of a 16-digit panel based on the humidity sensor using CQDs@NFCs for controlling electronics.

head.ce:title: A highly sensitive flexible humidity sensor based on a wafer-level composite material of carbon-quantum-dots@nanofiber clusters

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Microelectronics of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100029, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Microelectronics of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Microelectronics of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100029, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Institute of Microelectronics of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100029, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Microelectronics of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel composite material composed of carbon-quantum-dots@nanofiber clusters (CQDs@NFCs) is successfully developed in wafer level, taking it as a sensitive material, a flexible humidity sensor is also developed. Since the composite material is superhydrophilic and contains a super large number of nanopores of different sizes, capillary condensation is able to take place within the composite material at different relative humidity (RH) conditions. Taking advantage of the chemi-physisorption and the capillary condensation, such a sensing material helps to significantly improve sensitivity of the flexible sensor, especially at low RH conditions. Compared with the device adopting only nanofibers as the sensing material, this novel sensor offers a 4.3-time increament in sensitivity within a humidity range of 7%−59% RH, while maintains high sensitivity in the entire humidity range. This heightened sensitivity is crucial for monitoring low-level humidity with high accuracy, making the sensor highly promising for various applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Humidity is a ubiquitous parameter that holds great significance in various aspects of our lives [1,2]. Suitable relative humidity (RH) in surrounding environments contributes to human comfort, health and hygienic conditions [3–5]. Besides, proper humidity is crucial in various industries, such as semiconductors, electronics, food processing, and pharmaceuticals, as it can significantly impact the quality of products [6]. Additionally, maintaining suitable humidity levels is essential for preserving artworks and archives in museums or libraries [7]. In addition to the above industrial applications, there is a growing focus on the humidity of living environments, leading to rapid expansion in the application fields of humidity testing devices. These fields include real-time environment monitoring, human health, plant health, noncontact human-machine interface (HMI), and so on [8]. To meet these applications, achieving excellent sensitivity and a wide testing range is a prerequisite. Nevertheless, the low sensitivity of most current humidity sensors under low RH limits their practical applications [9]. Development of highly sensitive materials is believed to be crucial to increase the performance of humidity sensors, especially under low RH conditions.

To achieve high-performance humidity sensors, it is desirable to develop a method that can effectively prepare humidity-sensitive materials with numerous hydrophilic groups and extensive surface areas. In recent years, researchers have developed various sensing materials for humidity sensors [10,11]. Polymers, such as polyimide, copolymerized polymethyl methacrylate/polymerizing methacryloylamino propyl-trimethylammonium chloride (PMMA/PMAPTAC), and polyelectrolytes, have gained attention due to their hydrophilicity, ease of processing and integration [12,13]. However, polymer-based humidity sensors have limitations in terms of their small surface areas and narrow detection ranges, making them impractical for certain industrial and household applications. WaveFlex sensors, with their innovative designs, also offer potential in detecting RH in critical applications [14–18]. However, their practical use is restricted by the metal-introduced materials. In recent years, several carbon-based nanomaterials have been developed, such as graphene, carbon nanotubes, and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [19,20], etc. However, due to clustering of the nanomaterials during their preparation to form the humidity-sensitive layers, inhomogeneity of the materials becomes an issue. To overcome these limitations, two approaches have been employed to prepare sensitive materials and improve their sensitivity. The first approach involves preparation of the sensitive materials into nanostructures, which helps to introduce a larger surface area and thus can significantly enhance the adsorption capacity for water molecules. The other method involves modification of the sensitive materials with certain groups or nanostructures to increase active sites for adsorbing water molecules [21]. Notably, both approaches can enhance sensitivity of the sensors through improving adsorption capacity of sensitive materials. Nevertheless, in order to prepare structures with large surface areas, processes requiring high-end photolithography (e.g. E-beam lithography) and chemical synthesis are sometimes utilized, but these approaches are sometimes complicated and with high cost, besides, they are not compatible with CMOS processes for wafer-level fabrication [22,23].

In this work, a novel composite material composed of carbon-quantum-dots@nanofiber clusters (CQDs@NFCs) is prepared by wafer-level plasma etching and a subsequent step of solvent evaporation. Such a composite material exhibits an extensive area and numerous hydrophilic groups. With these features, the material is further used as a humidity sensitive material for constructing a flexible humidity sensor with high sensitivity and a wide sensing range. The prepared sensor exhibits excellent performance in various applications, including accurate environment humidity monitoring, finger proximity detection, plant transpiration detection, and diaper urination monitoring. Additionally, a humidity sensor array is fabricated for more complicated noncontact humidity sensing. It is expected that the prepared humidity sensor and the array can further be used in smart medical and wearable devices in the future.

CQDs, absolute ethanol (C2H6O), calcium chloride (CaCl2), sodium bromide (NaBr), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), potassium sulfate (K2SO4), potassium carbonate (K2CO3), lithium chloride (LiCl), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium acetate (CH3COOK), copper dichloride (CuCl2) and potassium chloride (KCl) are purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Co., Ltd. The conductive silver paste is obtained from Huaqiang Electronics Co., Ltd., China. Si substrates are purchased from Microtech Co., Ltd. in Suzhou, China. The transparent-flexible substrate is obtained from Fujikura Kasei Co., Ltd. Photosensitive Polyimide (PI) is provided by POME Technology Co., Ltd., China. A humidity pass filter is purchased from Dongguan PUW Material Co., Ltd. in China.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of NFs and CQDs@NFCs on flexible substrates
ce:section.ce:para: The process for preparing nanoforests (NFs) on a wafer-level flexible substrate is as follows. Firstly, a PI layer is spin-coated (Fig. S1a) and patterned on the flexible substrate (Fig. S1b). Subsequently, the PI patterns are subjected to oxygen plasma bombardment to form NFs (Fig. S1c). The plasma bombardment process involved parameters including an RF power of 200 W, a flowing rate of O2 at 120 sccm, and a duration of 30 minutes. In such a process, because of plasma repolymerization, nanoforests are generated [24–26]. Simultaneously, 100 mg of CQD powders were dissolved in 100 mL of ethanol, resulting in a CQD solution with a concentration of 1 mg/mL. Then the flexible substrate with NFs was immersed in the CQD solution for 1 min, after being lifted out of the solution, to speed up the evaporation of the solvent, the substrate was heated at 60 ℃ for 2 mins. To make sure the CQDs are grafted to the NFs, such a dipping-evaporation step is repeated for three times. During solvent evaporation, when vertical nanoforests are immersed in and then pulled out of a carbon quantum dots (CQDs) solution, capillary forces [27] cause the nanowires to clump together, forming nanowire bundles. With evaporation, the solvent is removed from the structures, leaving only the CQDs on surfaces of the nanowire bundles, in this way, CQDs@NFCs are formed (Fig. S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of humidity sensors based on the CQDs@NFCs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows the detailed process for fabricating a humidity sensor using CQDs@NFCs as the sensitive material. Firstly, interdigital electrodes (IDEs) are prepared by sputtering and patterning of an aluminum layer on the flexible film (detailed information is provided in Fig. S3). Then, a PI photoresist layer (8 μm thick) is spin-coated and photo-patterned on top of the IDEs (Fig. 1 I-II). After this step, the PI patterns can serve as a humidity-sensitive material, therefore, a PI-based flexible humidity sensor (PFHS) is obtained. Later on, with O2 plasma etching, NFs are formed in patterns where the PI photoresist existed. In the etching step, a mask film is stuck and aligned on surface of the substrate to protect the flexible substrate. After this step, the NFs can serve as another type of humidity-sensitive material, and thus a nanoforest-based flexible humidity sensor (NFHS) is achieved (Fig. 1 III-V). After that, CQDs are introduced in the NFs, forming the CQDs@NFCs on the IDEs, consequently, forming a novel flexible humidity sensor based on the CQDs@NFCs.

Morphologies of the NFCs are characterized using a scanning electron microscope (SEM) (SU8000, Hitachi, Japan). The CQDs are characterized using a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Talos F200X, Messtechnik GmbH, Germany) with an accelerating voltage of 100 kV. Hydrophilic groups are recorded with a Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (EoszooLite, Shimadzu, Japan). In this work, saturated aqueous solutions are utilized to test the performance of the prepared humidity sensor. The experimental setup for characterizing the sensors is presented in Supplementary Information as Fig. S4, and the procedure of the experiment adopting saturated aqueous solutions is listed in the Supplementary Information. Different RH conditions are provided by saturated aqueous solutions including CaCl2 (7% RH), LiCl (11% RH), CH3COOK (23% RH), MgCl2 (33% RH), K2CO3 (43% RH), NaBr (59% RH), CuCl2 (67% RH), NaCl (75% RH), KCl (85% RH) and K2SO4 (97% RH), respectively. It’s worth noting that the atmosphere temperature in the experiment is 25 ℃. A digital multimeter (Model DMM6500, Keithley, USA) is used to measure the capacitance responses of the humidity sensor, simultaneously, the results are displayed on screen of the multimeter, and later the data are analyzed. Multiple tests are taken to ensure the sensor’s response capability and repeatability under different RH conditions. The chamber humidity is monitored by a calibrated probe (HW5, Rotronic, Switzerland). The capacitance changes of the sensors are monitored at different RH conditions using the digital multimeter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphologies of the NFs and the CQDs@NFCs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 shows the SEM images of the NFs and the CQDs@NFCs. For comparison, images of a NFHS using NFs as the sensing material are presented, as shown in Fig. 2a, b. As shown in Fig. 2b, the NFs exhibit an average diameter of approximately 100 nm, a density of around 20 μm−2, and a height of approximately 5 μm. Fig. 2c shows photos of the prepared sensor based on the CQDs@NFCs, and Fig. S5 demonstrates the ability of batch preparation. Additionally, the insets in Fig. 2d and e show SEM images of the top and bottom structures of the CQDs@NFCs, respectively, and they present the dimensions of the capillary pores in the NFCs with different dimensions, which are mainly smaller than 100 nm, thus may lead to capillary condensation in different humid conditions (Fig. S6). Fig. S7 illustrates the process of capillary condensation taking place in the CQDs@NFCs, when the structures are placed in a humid chamber of environmental scanning electron microscopy (ESEM). Additionally, Fig. 2f displays a TEM image showing the distribution of the CQDs on surfaces of the NFCs and the lattice fringes are clearly visible, the high-resolution TEM image of the CQDs@NFCs composite further confirms the presence of CQDs (Fig. S8). Moreover, before taking into use of such materials, full and effective hydrophilicity characterization is needed. According to the FTIR results shown in Fig. S9a, abundant hydrophilic groups can be observed and the CQDs@NFCs exhibit a very small contact angle (inset of Fig. S9b and Video S1 in Supporting Information).
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.135493.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S1.
Video S1
.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of the humidity sensor based on CQDs@NFCs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 illustrates the measurement results of the flexible humidity sensor. Fig. 3a demonstrates the logarithmic response curve of the humidity sensor based on CQDs@NFCs, as the RH changes in a range of 7%−97%. Herein, the capacitance at 7% RH, which is 3.9 pF, is taken as the base for measuring the response of the sensor within the RH range from 11% to 97%. As illustrated in this figure, the capacitance of the humidity sensor increases obviously with the rise of RH, indicating its excellent humidity-sensing ability. With such a remarkable response of the sensor, the detection range of 7%−97% RH can be ensured. As in our experiment, the lowest RH the saturated aqueous solutions could provide was 7% RH. It should be noted that, it is possible for the sensor developed in this work to test even lower RH conditions, thus an even wide detection range can be achieved. Additionally, the sensor is tested using a humidity/temperature chamber (Fig. S10 and Fig. S11) to validate the sensor’s response capability and repeatability under different RH conditions.

Further, to evaluate the sensing performance of the three types of humidity sensors (the PFHS, the NFHS, and the device based on CQDs@NFCs), their capacitance responses at different relative humidities (7%−97% RH) were plotted in a logarithmic coordinate (Fig. 3b). As is shown in this figure, all these sensors can respond in such a RH range, compared to the other two devices, the one based on CQDs@NFCs has the largest sensitivity and the lowest deviation from the linear curve. Since the only difference among the three devices is the sensing material, therefore, it is reasonable to say that the composite material of CQDs@NFCs is able to improve sensitivity and reduce error of the sensor. This makes it an ideal material for humidity sensing. To precisely quantify the humidity sensitivity of the novel sensor, the logarithmic capacitance of the device is illustrated in Fig. 3c, as shown in this figure, the sensor responds linearly at various RHs, and in the range from 7% to 97% RH, an approximately 102 times of variation can be achieved, which highlights the exceptional sensitivity of the sensor in both high and low RH conditions. In addition, we conducted a linear regression analysis on the response curve for different humidity ranges, as illustrated in the inset of Fig. 3c, the humidity sensor based on CQDs@NFCs shows good linearity between the sensor response and RH with corresponding RH ranges of 7%−59% and 59%−97%. In contrast, although NFHS and PFHS also have distinct sensing performance, they are inferior to the device based on CQDs@NFCs. According to Fig. 3d, compared with the NFHS, the sensitivity of the novel sensor based on CQDs@NFCs is increased by 4.3 times in the 7%−59% RH range. Taking the responses within 7%−59% RH as an example, the novel sensor shows a prominent capacitance change of 39.1 pF (Fig. S12c) while the capacitance changes of NFHS and PFHS are only 8.3 pF and 2.5 pF, respectively (Fig. S12a,b), which directly evidences the best sensing performance of the CQDs@NFC-based humidity sensor. Fig. 3e demonstrates the repeatability of the sensor by measuring its response when exposed to a change in RH between 7% RH and 85% RH. These experimental results confirm that the sensor has good performance.

The structures of CQDs@NFCs own a large surface area and a large amount of hydrophilic groups, therefore, they have substantial active sites for absorbing water molecules. When the sensor based on the CQDs@NFCs is placed in a humid environment, with the large absorbing capacity of the CQDs@NFCs, the dielectric constant of the composite material increases significantly with the rising RH, as a result, the sensor's capacitance is increased accordingly.

For the composite material of CQDs@NFCs, the dielectric layer consists of CQDs, NFCs and water vapor, therefore, capacitance (C) of the capacitive humidity sensor can be described as
(1)
C = ε
A
4 k π d
Where variables ε, A, and d represent the effective dielectric constant, effective sensing area, and thickness of the dielectric layer, respectively. k refers to the electrostatic force constant. For this humidity sensor, the structural parameters A and d are constants, meaning that the capacitance is primarily determined by ε. Since the dielectric layer consists of CQDs, NFCs and water vapor, ε is a combination of the effective dielectric constants of these materials. The effective dielectric constant of the composite material can be defined using the General Lichterecker mixing rule [28].
(2)
ε =
V
CQDs
ε
CQDs
+
V
NFCs
ε
NFCs
+
V
w
ε
w

Here, ε CQDs, ε NFCs and ε w are the effective dielectric constants of CQDs (∼5.5–16.5), NFCs (∼3) and water vapor (∼80), respectively; V CQDs, V NFCs and V w are the volume fractions (for the composite material of CQDs@NFCs, there is V CQDs+V NFCs+V w=1) of CQDs, NFCs and water vapor, respectively.

For the humidity sensor based on the composite material of CQDs@NFCs, the adsorption process for water molecules is depicted in Fig. 4. When the humidity sensor is exposed to a dry-environment, ε w can be neglected since there exists the scarcity of water molecules in the dielectric layer [29]. In this case, ε consisits mainly of ε CQDs and ε NFCs. Since there are more active sites on surfaces of the CQDs@NFCs than the water molecules surrounding the structures, water molecules tend to be chemisorbed to the active sites around the NFCs and the CQDs by hydrogen bonding [30]. Specifically, as the CQDs are more hydrophilic than the NFCs, under the super-low humid condition, i.g. 7% RH, water molecules are more likely to be bonded to the CQDs first.

Then, with the gradual increase of RH, there comes more water molecules surrounding the CQDs@NFCs. Similarly, the water molecules are more likely to be chemisorbed to the CQDs first, and then, when all the active sites around the CQDs are occupied, the other molecules tend to graft to the hydrophilic groups around the NFCs also through hydrogen bonds [31], thus forming a hydroxyl layer around the composite material. Subsequently, as the amount of water vapor increases, the vapor pressure also rises. These additional water molecules are then physically adsorbed to the hydroxyl layer [32], and finally, the physically adsorbed monolayer converts to multiple water molecule layers. In short, with the synergistic functions of chemisorption and physisorption, the water molecules are adsorbed to the CQDs@NFCs (Fig. 4).

During the aforementioned chemisorption and physisorption processes, capillary condensation also occurs in the capillary pores existing in the composite material [33], the procedure is described in detail as follows. When the size of the capillary pores in the NFCs is extremely small, chemisorption of a small number of water molecules provided by a very low humidity condition will lead to a single layer of water molecules. Since the pores are very small, the condensed monolayer of water molecules occupies the inner surfaces of the pores, even without the function of the physisorption step. When the capillary pores in the NFCs are relatively larger, after chemisorption of a certain number of water molecules(determined by numerous active sites on the inner surfaces of the pores), physisorption is also required to form the multi-layer of water molecules, only in this way, a continuous water film can be formed on the inner surfaces of the pores [34,35]. That is to say, for a larger pore to achieve capillary condensation, a higher RH is required. Similarly, a much higher RH condition can cause condensation among the NFCs (with much larger space) ( Fig. 5).

After capillary condensation, the condensate can transfer along the surface of the NFCs to the side where there are fewer water molecules. For instance, when there is a rising in RH, there are more water molecules on the tops of the NFCs than that at the bottoms, therefore, the condensate is more likely to transfer from the tops to the bottoms along the NFCs, subsequently, the water molecules are enriched at the bottoms of the structures, where there distribute the IDEs. As a result, sensitivity of the sensor is able to be enlarged. On the contrary, when there is a decrease in RH, the condensate will transfer from the bottoms to the tops, which helps to accelerate the desportion of the water molecules.

With the prepared flexible humidity sensor based on CQDs@NFCs, we further developed a humidity monitoring system adopting a flexible circuit board for different applications, as illustrated in Fig. 6a. In addition to the flexible humidity sensor for data collection, such a system also contains a data processing module, a wireless transmission module and an APP, as shown in Fig. 6b. The data processing module converts capacitance signals to voltage signals, and with an anolog-to-digital converter, digital RH data can be provided. The wireless module adopting a bluetooth chip (ZX-D30) is used for transmitting data from the system to computers or mobile phones for further analysis. To analyze these data and to finally display the results, an APP is developed. On the interface of the APP, it displays both digital values of RH and time-varying dynamic curves of the detected RH.

With such a system, fast monitoring of humidity in surrounding environment is demonstrated, as illustrated in Fig. 6c, such a system allows individuals to monitor the RH of their surroundings in real-time, enabling them to take appropriate measures for a comfortable living environment. As an illustration, a human finger functioning as a moist source gradually approaches the sensor array, the sensor in accordance, responds to the RH variation, thus it can monitor the RH in real time through the capacitance changes (Fig. 6d and Videos S2). To further demonstrate the high resolution sensing ability of the humidity sensor, the sensor is approached to a plant leaf, as shown in Fig. 6e and Videos S2, significant capacitance changes can be observed. As a proof-of-concept of high sensitivity in a wide RH range, we conducted an experiment where the sensor was attached to a baby diaper for urination monitoring (Fig. 6f and Videos S3). To mimic the urination of a baby, water is injected onto the baby diaper. To protect the humidity sensor based on CQDs@NFCs, from contamination, scratches, and direct water contact, we covered a water-resistant humidity pass filter on the flexible circuit board (Fig. S13). Upon contact with the humidity sensor, the water molecules can trigger the smartphone to emit a sound when the output of the sensor exceeds the predetermined threshold (Video S3, Supporting Information). This immediate notification can effectively alert the caregiver to replace the diaper, ultimately improving comfort of the wearers.
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.135493.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S2.
Video S2
.
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.135493.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S3.
Video S3
.
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.135493.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S4.
Video S4
.

A smart wireless finger-controlled HMI system has also been developed by taking advantages of the humidity sensor based on CQDs@NFCs. The system consists of a 16-digital arrayed (4 × 4) noncontact panel adopting such a humidity sensor, signal processing, and wireless transmitting circuits. When a human finger approaches the humidity sensor-based panel, the raw output signals are converted into square waves by the processing circuit. The wireless signal receiver then receives and transmits the signal to another MCU (Fig. S14). To demonstrate the practical applications of the HMI system, an array of light-emitting diodes (LEDs) were used. The LEDs can be turned on or off by using this HMI system. This demonstrates the potential use of the smart HMI humidity sensor in intelligent robotics.

In conclusion, we developed a highly sensitive flexible humidity sensor adopting CQDs@NFCs as its humidity-sensitive material, such a composite material is prepared in wafer level simply by plasma etching and a subsequent solvent evaporation step. Because the CQDs@NFCs have an extensive surface area, numerous functional groups, and a large number of capillary pores with different sizes, chemi-physisorption of water molecules and capillary condensation take place in the composite material. Based on such a composite material, a humidity sensor is prepared and is proved to have effective capacitance change upon exposure to low RH conditions. On the basis of its superior sensing performance, the prepared sensor achieves accurate monitoring of environment humidity, finger proximity, plant transpiration and urination in diapers. In addition, a humidity sensor array based on the CQDs@NFCs is also fabricated to explore the potential applications in noncontact humidity sensing.

Xu Shaohang: Investigation. Zhou Na: Investigation, Funding acquisition. Li Mao: Investigation. Zhao Yuefang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Mao Haiyang: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Huang Chengjun: Supervision. Shi Meng: Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported in part by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 62201567), Youth Innovation Promotion Association, Chinese Academy of Sciences (Grant No. 2018153 and 2022117), Key-Area Research and Development Program of Guangdong Province (Grant No. 2019B010117001), and State Key Laboratory of Dynamic Test Jointly Built by Province and Ministry Open Fund (Grant No. 2022-SYSJJ-07).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.135493.

.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-performance porous MIM-type capacitive humidity sensor realized via inductive coupled plasma and reactive-ion etching

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Qiang, C. Wang, M.-Q. Liu, K.K. Adhikari, N.-Y. Kim, Z. Yao, High-performance porous MIM-type capacitive humidity sensor realized via inductive coupled plasma and reactive-ion etching, Sens. Actuators B Chem. 258 (2018) 704-714.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Full-textile wireless flexible humidity sensor for human physiological monitoring

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Size effect in ion transport through angstrom-scale slits

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Esfandiar，B. Radha，F.C. Wang，Q. Yang，S. Hu，S. Garaj，R.R. Nair，A.K. Geim，K. Gopinadhan, Size effect in ion transport through angstrom-scale slits, Science 358 (2017) 511-513.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yuefang Zhao is currently pursuing her Ph.D. degree at the Institute of Microelectronics of the Chinese Academy of Sciences. She received her M.S. degree from North University of China in 2021. Her research interests include MEMS/NEMS devices.

Mao Li is currently pursuing his Ph.D. degree at the Institute of Microelectronics of the Chinese Academy of Sciences. He received his B.E. degree from Tsinghua University (China) in 2019. His research interests include nanomaterials and MEMS/NEMS devices.

Shaohang Xu is currently pursuing his Ph.D. degree at the Institute of Microelectronics of the Chinese Academy of Sciences. He received his M.S. degree from the University of Chinese Academy of Sciences (China) in 2020. His research interests include MEMS/NEMS devices.

Meng Shi is currently pursuing her Ph.D. degree at the Institute of Microelectronics of the Chinese Academy of Sciences. She received her M.S. degree from the University of Chinese Academy of Sciences (China) in 2018. Her research interests include nanomaterial-based SERS sensors, MEMS devices, and their applications.

Na Zhou is currently a senior engineer at the Institute of Microelectronics of Chinese Academy of Sciences. She received her Ph.D. degree from the University of Chinese Academy of Sciences (China) in 2013. Her recent research interests include plasma etching, the semiconductor process of advanced CMOS and MEMS devices, MEMS/NMES devices and their applications.

Chengjun Huang is a professor at Institute of Microelectronics of Chinese Academy of Sciences. He received his Ph. D. degree from Microelectronics, Huazhong University of Science and Technology (China) in 2006. From 2007–2013, he worked as a senior researcher in IMEC, Belgium. His current research interests are the interdisciplinary collaborative and sustainable innovations, with focuses on both microelectronic and biomedical fields: research on novel chips, sensor devices, equipment, and intelligent health management systems for healthcare and medical applications.

Haiyang Mao is currently a professor at Institute of Microelectronics of Chinese Academy of Sciences. She received her B.E. and M.S. degrees from North University of China in 2004 and 2007. She received her Ph.D. degree (2011) from Peking University (China) in Microelectronics and Solid Electronics. Her research interests include MEMS devices and nanomaterial-based sensors.
